# Infrastructure as Code

The `infra/` folder contains infrastructure as code (IaC) configurations for provisioning and managing cloud resources. We use [Pulumi](https://www.pulumi.com/) via the [uv toolchain](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/languages-sdks/python/#uv).

Each Pulumi [project](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/concepts/projects) has at minimum two [stacks](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/concepts/stacks), one each for **staging** and **production**, in addition to personal dev stacks. Mono-project repos place their project at `infra/app`. Multi-project repos place projects under `infra/projects/`.

## Project Layout

### Mono-project (default)

Most repos should use a single project at `infra/app`:

```
infra/
├── common/
|   └── libs/            # Common Pulumi libs synced from fde-core
└── app/
    ├── __main__.py              # Pulumi program defining your infrastructure
    ├── Pulumi.yml               # Project settings and template metadata
    ├── Pulumi.dev-<name-a>.yml  # Allows dev `name-a` to test changes in their own stack
    ├── Pulumi.dev-<name-b>.yml  # Allows dev `name-b` to test changes in their own stack
    ├── Pulumi.staging.yml       # Configuration specific to the `staging` stack
    ├── Pulumi.production.yml    # Configuration specific to the `production` stack
    └── pyproject.toml           # Python dependencies (Pulumi SDK, Azure-specific SDKs, etc.)
```

### Multi-project

For repos that need multiple independent Pulumi projects, create an `infra/projects/` directory and place each project under it:

```
infra/
├── common/
|   └── libs/
└── projects/
    ├── app/
    |   ├── __main__.py
    |   ├── Pulumi.yml
    |   ├── Pulumi.staging.yml
    |   ├── Pulumi.production.yml
    |   └── pyproject.toml
    └── platform/
        ├── __main__.py
        ├── Pulumi.yml
        ├── Pulumi.staging.yml
        ├── Pulumi.production.yml
        └── pyproject.toml
```

All projects are discovered automatically by our CI/CD workflows — any directory directly under `infra/projects/` containing a `Pulumi.yml` is treated as a project.

### Mono vs. multi-project: when to use which

> **Recommendation:** Stay with a single `infra/app` project unless you have a clear and pressing need for multiple projects.

A single project is simpler to reason about, has a single deployment lifecycle, and avoids the overhead of managing cross-project dependencies. Most teams should start here and only split when they encounter concrete pain.

Reasons you might consider splitting into multiple projects:
- **Independent deployment lifecycles** — one project changes frequently while another is mostly static
- **Blast radius isolation** — a failed deployment in one project shouldn't block or affect another

Trade-offs of multi-project:
- Each project is deployed **in parallel with no dependency ordering** — if project B depends on outputs from project A, you must ensure A is deployed first (e.g. by merging and deploying changes in the correct order)
- More configuration files to maintain (each project has its own `Pulumi.yml`, stack configs, `pyproject.toml`)
- Production deploys require choosing which project(s) to deploy

For more guidance, see Pulumi's documentation on [organizing projects and stacks](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/guides/basics/organizing-projects-stacks).

## Deployment Workflow

```
pull request
├── pulumi preview --stack staging (for each project)
├── wait for pull request approval
└── merge pull request
    ├── start staging deploy
    ├── pulumi up --stack staging (for each project, in parallel)
    └── start production deploy
        ├── pulumi preview --stack production (for each project)
        ├── wait for plan approval (per project)
        └── pulumi up --stack production (for each project)
```

* Open a pull request against `main`
  - When a pull request is opened, we generate a [preview](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/cli/commands/pulumi_preview) of the changes that will be made against the **`staging`** environment via the [`pulumi-preview-staging.yml`](/.github/workflows/pulumi-preview-staging.yml) workflow. This is posted as a comment on the pull request.
* Merge the pull request
* Deploy to **`staging`** (automatic)
  - When the pull request is merged, we automatically deploy to the **`staging`** environment via the [`pulumi-deploy-staging.yml`](/.github/workflows/pulumi-deploy-staging.yml) workflow. All discovered projects are deployed in parallel.
* Deploy to **`production`** (manual)
  - After the **`staging`** deployment is complete and you've tested your changes in the staging environment, you can manually start the **`production`** deployment via the [`pulumi-deploy-production.yml`](/.github/workflows/pulumi-deploy-production.yml) workflow.
  - Click the "Run workflow" dropdown on the right-hand side of the workflow page. Fill out the fields:
    * **`Use workflow from`**: `Branch: main`
    * **`Project`**: Name of a project under `infra/` (e.g. `app`, `platform`), or `all` to deploy everything.
    * **`(Optional) The commit sha to deploy`**: `123abc...`
      - If not provided, the HEAD of the default branch (`main`) will be deployed.
  - Click the green "Run workflow" button to start the deployment.
  - At some point your deployment will pause and wait for your approval of the Pulumi preview to proceed. The preview is posted as a new GitHub issue; the link to the issue will be shown in the workflow logs.
  - Once approved, we proceed with the deployment to the **`production`** environment.

## Dev Stacks

Each developer working on a Pulumi project should have their own [dev stack](https://www.pulumi.com/blog/iac-best-practices-enabling-developer-stacks-git-branches/#using-developer-stacks). This allows each dev to iterate freely on both applications and infrastructure in the same cloud environment without the risk of collisions.

The developer stack configuration files are checked into version control and live alongside the `staging` and `production` stacks:

```
infra/
└── app/
    ├── __main__.py
    ├── Pulumi.yml
    ├── Pulumi.dev-charlie.yml  # targets the `dev` Azure subscription from local
    ├── Pulumi.dev-nicole.yml   # targets the `dev` Azure subscription from local
    ├── Pulumi.staging.yml      # targets the `staging` Azure subscription from GitHub Actions
    ├── Pulumi.production.yml   # targets the `production` Azure subscription from GitHub Actions
    └── pyproject.toml
```

Because we all share a single Azure subscription for testing, all dev stacks should have a [Pulumi naming config](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/concepts/resources/names/#autonaming-configuration) to prefix resource names:

**`Pulumi.dev-charlie.yml`**
```yaml
config:
  pulumi:autonaming:
    pattern: charlie${project}${name}${hex(6)}
```

**`Pulumi.dev-nicole.yml`**
```yaml
config:
  pulumi:autonaming:
    pattern: nicole${project}${name}${hex(6)}
```

This will allow multiple developers to create copies of your Pulumi project within the same Azure subscription without naming conflicts.
This also makes the resources easier to identify in the Azure portal.

### Create and manage a dev stack

The below instructions assume your terminal is located in the directory containing the Pulumi project you want to test (`infra/app`).

To create and deploy your dev stack:

#### Login to the **FDE dev** Azure subscription
```bash
az account set --subscription f8fa7ae2-2d73-40d7-b498-425ff8833843
```

#### Login to the local Pulumi backend
```bash
pulumi login --local
```

> [!NOTE]
> This uses a local file to manage the Pulumi state.

#### Set an encryption key
```bash
export PULUMI_CONFIG_PASSPHRASE=$(openssl rand -hex 32)
echo "export PULUMI_CONFIG_PASSPHRASE=$PULUMI_CONFIG_PASSPHRASE" >> ~/.bashrc
```

> [!TIP]
> We recommend setting this value in your `~/.bashrc` file. When setting up your stack for the first time, Pulumi will add an [encryption salt](https://www.pulumi.com/docs/iac/concepts/projects/stack-settings-file/#encryptionsalt) to your config file. This salt + your encryption key are used to encrypt/decrypt any sensitive values in your config file. See [Working with Secrets](https://www.pulumi.com/tutorials/building-with-pulumi/secrets/) for more details.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> You should commit your modified `Pulumi.dev-<name>.yml` file with the encryption salt to version control.

> [!TIP]
> If you lose your passphrase, you will want to remove your stack from the state (`pulumi stack rm dev-<name> --force`) and start over. Make sure to clean up any dangling resources in the Azure Portal (typically by deleting the resource group that was created by your dev stack, which will delete all child resources).

#### Add the stack to the state
```bash
pulumi stack select dev-<name> --create
```

> [!NOTE]
> This only creates the stack in your local Pulumi backend. It does not create any resources in Azure.

> [!TIP]
> You can also copy an existing dev's config with `pulumi stack init dev-<name> --copy-config-from dev-<other-name>`.
> **Make sure to update the naming convention prefix to your own name in the created file.**

#### Deploy the stack
```bash
pulumi up
```
